The biggest reason the rich get richer (besides compounding) is they avoid things that don’t make enough money.

The more money you have, the more selective you are about which things are worth doing because fewer things move the needle for you.

If you make decisions like you have a lot of money, you make more faster.

Opportunity vehicle selection, rather than pure effort, is why they get such high returns.

Alex Hormozi is a first-generation Iranian-American entrepreneur, investor, and philanthropist. In 2013, he started his first brick & mortar business. Within three years, he successfully scaled his business to six locations. He then sold his locations to transition to the turnaround business. From there he spent two years turning 32+ brick & mortar businesses around using the same model that made his privately owned locations successful.

After that experience, he packaged his process into a licensing model which scaled rapidly in 4 years. Over that same four-year period, he founded and scaled three other companies to $120M+ in cumulative sales across four different industries (software, service, e-commerce, and brick & mortar) without taking on outside capital. He has scaled and exited 7 companies. His most notable exit was his majority sale of his licensing company for $46.2M in 2021.

He transitioned from CEO to the owner/shareholder position in these companies in 2020, and founded Acquisition.com as a way to invest his own wealth (both monetary and intellectual capital) into other businesses. The portfolio is based on his investment thesis focusing on asset-light, high cash flow, sales-focused service and digital products businesses.

He is known for his expertise in customer acquisition and monetization.

He donates much of his free time to advancing equal access to education and encouraging entrepreneurship in underprivileged communities.

Alex graduated from Vanderbilt University Magna Cum Laude in three years with a BS in Human & Organizational Development with a focus on Corporate Strategy. He worked as a management consultant for a boutique strategy firm for two years after graduating before starting his first business.

17 lessons you must learn if you want to be a top 1% salesperson:

1. People want to believe you.
They inherently want to buy.
You need to help their logical brains justify that emotional decision.

2. Selling happens before you ask for the sale.
Closing begins after.

3. It’s easier to handle obstacles than objections.
Obstacles are when YOU disagree with the prospect.
Objections are when THEY disagree with you.

Diffuse obstacles early, so they do not become objections and blow up the sale in the close.

4. Expect and plan for NO.
It is not failure; it is expected. 
If they could decide without you, you would be unnecessary.

No is the job.

Stop being surprised.

5. If you didn’t get a gasp from the price tag—
You didn’t go high enough.
Raise your prices.

6. Selling correctly is the first step to helping your prospects.

Your first impression and the expectations you set will dictate the relationship.

7. Selling is helping prospects make decisions to help themselves.
The objective is not to get them to buy but to get them to decide.

8. Prioritize helping your prospects—
Not the sale.

It’s not about you.

9. Seek to understand, not argue.
You cannot influence someone you do not understand.

10. Closing is a dance, not a fight.

11. Selling is a transference of belief over a bridge of trust.

Therefore, you must have the belief first, then build trust to transfer it.

12. You can only build trust if you genuinely want to help.
Why? Because humans are exceptionally good at sniffing out intention.

No ‘hack’ will replace the trust built from a genuine desire to help.

13. Belief and trust are continuums, not binaries.

It’s not WHETHER they trust or believe you, but HOW MUCH that dictates the sale.

Act accordingly.

14. Closers ask hard questions—
Everyone in their lives lies to them, especially themselves.

You must ask the profound truth questions they are afraid of answering.

15. The person who cares the most about the prospect wins the deal.

16. Record your sales calls. Always.
You cannot improve what you do not get feedback on.

17. Power is the ability to direct or influence people or events.

“Passion” comes from the Latin “passio” which means “suffering” and “endurance.” 

So people think that following your passion means doing something you love, when it really means finding something that you are willing to suffer for.
People wildly underestimate how effective trying way harder than your competition works.

You are using the excuse of lack of passion to disguise your inability to handle difficulty.

Your passion only exists in the vague. even if you start a business around your “passion” 95% of what you do everyday will NOT be your passion.

You’ll just have brief moments where you do that specific thing (if at all) and assuming that thing never changes (which it will).

So if you know that:
1) Unless you get good at your passion, you will have to do things you like less to pay the bills.
2) As soon as you are good at your passion, demand will outstrip your supply of time and 95% of what you do will not be “the thing you love” but stuff you do to support the thing you love, which you may indeed “not love”
3) The 5% of your passion that’s leftover will only be there if your passion doesn’t change (which it will)

Which means the vast majority of your life you will not be doing things you are passionate about. And in the tiny instance you do, it’s likely short lived.

I say all this to say, delaying your pursuit because you’re waiting to find your passion is a fools errand.

Find something people value. Do that thing even though it sucks. Realize there is no greener grass. It all sucks. But it sucks less when you’re good. And the best way to get good is to get started.

My mother died in an unexpected accident at her home on Friday night in Baltimore. 
I want to take a brief moment to honor her life.

She was admitted into the first ever female class at John’s Hopkins (a huge accomplishment, because before that, it was men only).

She ran a private eye surgery practice for years.

She went to Africa multiple times to operate on entire villages to literally heal the blind.

She helped anyone who didn’t have money - despite never having much of her own.

She gave her life to Christ when she was 42, and never looked back. She lived in God’s grace.

For the few who knew her, you know she was incredibly intelligent, kind, and unyielding in her faith and values.

She also loved me fiercely despite her difficulty showing it.

But she was always proud. Always available. Always genuine. And truly did not care what anyone thought besides Jesus.

Also…

She was a great stay at home movie date. We’d get double features on the weekends. And I’d get to pick one candy.

She was a great cook and liberal with her use of butter. I only found out as an adult that the proper way to make Mac n cheese was not simply “melt a stick of butter then add noodles & powder.” This method also applied to eggs, steak, most other things worth eating.

She could hold her liquor like a champ and was always down for a good time.

On her good days - she was honestly wonderful to be around.

That being said, she was also a terrible driver. She was disorganized, clumsy, could not remember where she put her keys, wallet, phone, shoes or whatever she was looking for - ever.

And she’d argue about anything.

And that was Florence.

No matter what you thought of her, she was consistent. And she bent for no one.

Florence was a ball of goodness that struggled to deal with the world around her. She suffered her own demons. And near the end was in great mental and physical pain.

Now that she has passed, her pain has ended. And she can finally reunite with her Creator. 

I hope her worldview is right and she is welcomed home in His arms with a message we’d all want: “well done my good and faithful servant.”

10 Lessons from Our $100M Book Launch

Everyone’s asking how we pulled off the most significant book launch in history. 
Here’s what actually worked:

1. Single Focus Ownership. Here’s what we did differently: instead of assigning this as a side project to existing team members, we brought in people who were successful in their domains and made it their primary job. Most companies fail to launch because they assign massive projects to people already overwhelmed with their day jobs. 

2. Conviction Beats Marketing Every Time. Alex didn’t need to “fake it till he made it” because he’d lived this book for years. When the person selling actually believes the product will change lives, it shows up in every conversation, email, text, ad. You can’t manufacture that energy with better copy or flashier ads. Conviction is earned through the work you do alone when nobody is watching.

3. You Can’t Scale Past Your Leadership Ceiling. We couldn’t have pulled this off a year ago, our team wasn’t ready. This required lawyers to protect our IP, finance teams to model massive risk, people to forecast headcount spikes, marketing to crank out 2,000+ ads, and executives to make split-second decisions under intense pressure. Most launches fail because the leadership team isn’t mature enough to handle what success actually demands.

4. Hope Isn’t a Strategy, Redundancy Is. We didn’t cross our fingers and hope for the best. Power outage? Generators ready. Generators fail? Starlink backup. Internet dies? Hotspots queued up. Every critical point had multiple backup plans because “winging it” is how you turn a $100M opportunity into an expensive lesson.

5. Purpose > Finances. The world record motivated our team more than any bonus structure ever could. Big, visible goals tap into something money can’t touch - the desire to be part of something legendary. Financial incentives work up to a point, then purpose takes over.

We broke a Guinness World Record.

I’m not writing this to celebrate us. I’m writing this because somewhere right now, someone is staring at their own impossible dream, convinced it can’t be done.

They’re looking at their idea - the one that keeps them up at night, the one everyone says is “too big” or “unrealistic” - and they’re about to give up.

We did this for them.

And let me be clear: we had every reason to quit too. This year was very hard. We had to overcome a lot both in business and personal lives. There were many conversations of “Is this the right call? Should we wait? Should we keep pushing?” 

But here’s what we discovered: You don’t have to silence the doubts. You just have to work as if they’re not true.

We had doubts and built the systems anyway. We questioned ourselves and executed with precision anyway. We wondered if it was possible and planned as if it was inevitable anyway.

Yes, we broke a record. Yes, the operational excellence was extraordinary. Yes, Alex led like the f**king champion he is, and every single one of our team executed at a level that still gives me chills.

But the real victory isn’t in the record books. It’s in what we just proved is possible when ordinary people feel the fear and do the extraordinary work anyway.

Every entrepreneur watching this now knows something they didn’t know yesterday… your doubts don’t disqualify you.

The gap between dreaming and achieving isn’t fearlessness - it’s working with CONVICTION while the fear is still there. It’s building as if success is certain even when it feels impossible.

We didn’t conquer our doubts. We outworked them.

And now they can too.

Our Guinness World Record isn’t the end goal it’s the permission slip for everyone else to stop letting fear make their decisions.

I thought I was just documenting what I'd learned from 15 years in business.

Turns out, I was discovering patterns I'd never seen before.

Like this:
Every successful business follows the same 4-step sequence:

1 - Get customers cheaper than competitors

2 - Make more money from each customer

3 - Get customers to buy repeatedly

4 - Reinvest profits to get more customers

It sounds simple. But most businesses only do step 1. 

They spend all their time trying to get customers cheaper.

The smart businesses focus on steps 2-4. They make more money from each customer, so they can afford to pay more to get customers.

It's like having a bigger advertising budget than everyone else. Instantly.

Before I met @leilahormozi, I never cracked $3,000,000 per year. 24 months after, I took home $17,000,000 in personal income.

Some business lessons I’ve learned from her in no order:

— Never stifle a generous impulse. Whether it’s a gift or praise. The moment you think it, send it.
— You never regret taking a night to sleep on an emotional response.
— “Stress doesn’t mean there’s anything wrong. It just means you’re alive.”
— “You can feel bad and choose to do nothing about it.”
— “Long term >. I know what we’re going to do by the time we’re dead.”
— “The quality of your business is directly proportional to the quality of the people you hire which is directly proportional to your character.”
— “Be fast to extend trust and even faster to take it back.”
— “Don’t require three strikes to get someone out of your life. If someone shows you their colors, believe them the first time.”
— “Punishment only gets short term obedience. Praise gets long term loyalty.”
— “You cannot ask anyone for things you don’t already give them.” Patience, trust, etc.
— “Be fiercely competitive with yourself. Ignore everyone else.”
— “Listen to feedback the first time. If it makes you better, change it now, no matter how much it hurts.”
— “The best operators take action immediately on tasks. They don’t let anything sit on their desk. They are delegation and accountability machines. NOT “systems” people.”
— “People will care about you if you act like you care about them. And the easiest way to act like you care about them, is to actually care about them.”

She has been there since I made it. Lost it. Made it. Lost it again. And made it a third time.

The yin to my yang. Moderately successful on our own. And much stronger together.

And maybe this will be just one droplet against the ocean of gender wars.

I like my wife. She likes me. We work well together. We’re both better off teaming up.

In a world of division, there’s still room for union. At the very least, in your own home.

-One man’s .02.

My cat Bill died a few years ago from a sudden heart thing at barely two years old. (Don’t worry - there’s a happy reframe)

I was sad in the moment and tried to figure out why.

I realized at the core of it - I felt it was unfair that he died at two years old. 

Then I thought to myself “what a wild expectation to make of the universe—my cat must live the normal length or else!”

So I wondered if I could reframe his death for myself. 

What if all cats only lived six months…? and I got to hang with him for two years

Four times the norm! What luck! 

Reframing it this way made me feel grateful for the time I had and grateful he got to live so long(!)

He lived a long good life. 

A useful frame for reframing a painful experience: “what would have to be true for me to be grateful for this?” 

Nothing changed about his actual lifespan except for my (now exceeded) expectations for it. 

One man’s perspective. Use if helpful.

Throughout my life many people told me to “relax”. And I couldn’t.

I’ve never been an easy going guy. And I likely never will be.

But thinking back on the many different people who told me to “relax”…a recent thought came into mind.

Greatness isn’t laid back. 
It isn’t easy going. 
It isn’t relaxed.

It’s jealous. It’s obsessive. It’s demanding.

It required me to sleep on my gym floor, live on the road, and lose everything I had (twice).

It required me to quit my job, leave home, enter a new industry where I knew nothing and no one.

It required me to use up all my savings, make new partnerships, and break old ones.

It required me to get rejected by strangers, have money stolen, and get betrayed by friends.

And today, it requires me to work longer hours than I did even in the beginning, with fewer days off, and do it with a smile.

I still work like my life depends on it. Because I believe - it does.

My point: be excited that few people want or even have the ability to go hard. More for the few. More for those who hunt, fight, and persevere. It means more for you. More for me.

People think they need “work-life balance.”

Work-life balance is a great goal—if you’re okay with being balanced in the middle between the best and everyone else. 

But if you want to be the best, it’s not going to be enough. Greatness is just too fickle. She demands too much. And she doesn’t let you split your attention. She won’t let you coast. She won’t let you “just relax.”

Because the moment you do, she’ll leave you for someone who will give her more.

Whatever you’re not willing to sacrifice to keep her, someone else is.

TLDR: I want to achieve great things. And great things have a great price. And that price never feels, looks, or pretends to be…relaxed.

So, my message to anyone reading this:

If you want what you say you want, being “relaxed” won’t get you there. Being willing to sacrifice more for longer than anyone else…will.

And don’t expect anyone else to understand. Because if they could, they’d be right there with you.

Play by your own rules.

My dad used to tell me:

“I don’t play a game unless I know I’m going to win”

And some might take that as trying to only go after safe bets. 

But he meant it - and I took it - to mean something entirely different.

What he was articulating - in his way - was the idea of having “unfair” (not unethical) advantages in a game.

It’s a tiny thing, but it’s something I continue to give more and more thought to. 

“How can I rig this game so I can’t lose?”

In one way - it’s about eliminating downside. 

In the other - it’s about maximizing upside. 

And as a mental framework when approaching investments in a business - it’s one I use a lot. 

How can we make it so we can’t lose?

How can we make it is winning is inevitable?

The big variable that confounds results is a four letter word - Time. 

What is winning?
When do we measure it?

And most times - when people talk about winning - they talk about it on different timeliness. And that’s what creates confusion.

The nice thing is though - if you want a win tomorrow - it’s gonna be hard to engineer and there’s a lot of competition. See: Day-trading and Jim Simmons. 

But if you’re willing to expand the time horizon you can engineer most games to be un-loseable. 

It’s because there’s little competition when you compete on a 20 year time horizon. So there’s plenty of room for big obvious moves.

Then, you just gotta give time, time.

But I work the whole time. 

Hard to do unless you have some time operating guidelines. 

Here are some of mine:

#1:
Social obligations, aren’t obligations. 

Makes it easy to avoid the many hour long social events that eat up half a day. They add up fast.

#2:
Just because someone reaches out doesn’t mean you owe them a reply.

#3:
Unless someone has a specific reason to meet…
“connect” “pick your brain” “common interests” “see if we have synergies” are all code for “I want to take time from you because I haven’t done any thinking about how something could actually benefit us both. Avoid.

#4
No meetings before noon means no meetings before noon. 

Self-explanatory. If you say it, do it.

#5
“If you can’t make that time, then I guess we won’t be talking.”

If you don’t respect your time, expect no one else to.

“To make money, you need to provide value to your customer.”

That’s the standard advice, right?

Only one problem, what does that even mean?

I believe that there are 4 drivers to creating value:

#1 - Dream outcome

This is the expression of the feelings and experiences the prospect has envisioned in their mind. In other words, it’s the gap between their current reality and their dreams.

Our goal is to accurately depict that dream back to them, so they feel understood, and then explain how our vehicle (product or service) will get them there.

#2 - Perceived Likelihood of Achievement

People pay for certainty. They 
VALUE certainty. I call this “the perceived likelihood of achievement.

In other words, how likely does the prospect believe it is that they will achieve the result they are looking for if they make this purchase?

For example, how much would you pay to be a plastic surgeons 10,000th patient versus their first?

Probably A LOT more.

While the service you would be receiving would be the same, the thing that changes is your perceived likelihood of getting what you want.

#3 - Time Delay

This is the time between a client buying and receiving the promised benefit.

There are 2 elements to this driver of value:
- Long-term outcome
- Short-term experience

The thing people BUY is the long-term outcome (aka their “dream outcome”). 

The thing that makes them stay long enough to get that long-term outcome is the short-term experience.

These are the little milestones a prospect sees along the way that shows them they are on the right path. 

#4 - Effort and sacrifice

This is what the thing “costs” the customer in ancillary costs, aka “other costs accrued along the way.” This can be both tangible and intangible costs.

Decreasing the effort and sacrifice, or at least the perceived effort and sacrifice, can massively boost the appeal of your product or service.

—

When you’re creating value, the goal is to increase the “Dream Outcome” and the “Perceived Likelihood of Achievement.” 

You want to decrease “Time Delay” and the “Effort and Sacrifice” for the prospect.

This gets you “The Value Equation.”

**Only one person can be in the angry boat**

The biggest lesson I learned working for a fur coat dealer at 18 years old.

You have to fight fire with fire. But not the way people think. 

Angry people want to feel validated.

By telling them not to be angry they feel even more invalidated and get angrier.

So - you have to hop in the angry boat with them. You have to get angrier about whatever they were upset about than they were.

Then, they’ll try and calm YOU down telling YOU it wasn’t that big of a deal.

And so by going against your instincts to minimize the thing they’re upset about you can get them to do it for you.

A priceless lesson for dealing with angry customers or just angry people in general. Be angrier about the injustice than they are.
